name: Reflected methods
statement_text:
The reflection of the reflection of a Runge–Kutta method is
the original method.
  If a method satisfies some of the simplifying assumptions introduced in
Subsection 321, then we consider the possibility that the reflection of the
method satisfies corresponding conditions. To enable us to express these
                                   
connections conveniently, we write B(η),  
                                         C(η),  
                                                D(η)        ζ) to represent
                                                      and E(η,
B(η), C(η), D(η) and E(η, ζ), respectively, but with reference to the reflected
method. We then have:

variables.name: reflection
variables.definition: Given a Runge-Kutta method with tableau (c, A, b), its reflection is defined by the tableau with coefficients b_j - c_i and b_i - a_{ij}.

variables.name: simplifying assumptions
variables.definition: Conditions such as B(η), C(η), D(η), and E(η, ζ) introduced in Subsection 321, which relate to order conditions for Runge-Kutta methods.

variables.name: B(η), C(η), D(η), E(η, ζ)
variables.definition: Notations for the simplifying assumptions (e.g., order conditions) for a Runge-Kutta method.

variables.name: B̂(η), Ĉ(η), D̂(η), Ê(η, ζ)
variables.definition: The same simplifying assumptions but applied to the reflected method.

equations.content: The tableau for the reflection of a Runge-Kutta method, with entries b_j - c_i and b_i - a_{ij}.

preamble: The text discusses the reflection of a Runge-Kutta method, defined by transforming its tableau coefficients. The theorem states that reflecting a method twice returns the original method, and considers how simplifying assumptions (like order conditions) transfer to the reflected method.
